quote: Okay. So and does that garage have its own meter?
stewart_read: The rep correctly identifies that meter configuration is a product-fit diagnostic — a no-meter garage changes the install design entirely. This is a smart instinct. The failure is that the rep gets the answer ('No. It doesn't.') and moves on without flagging whether Ion can actually serve a no-meter structure. The question was asked; the implication was never resolved.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have known Ion can handle no-meter installs and moved on deliberately. No evidence supports this — the rep never confirmed it aloud.
coaching_implication: Manager should ask the rep: what did you do with the 'no meter' answer? If the rep doesn't have a clear answer about Ion's capability on no-meter detached structures, that's a product knowledge gap to close before the next similar call. The schema's cross_sell_signals section lists secondary-structure solar as TBD — this call is a live example of why that gap needs Kenny's input.

quote: And then what utility company are you with, Larry?
stewart_read: The rep asks utility company — a verify item — but never follows with the average bill question. The natural sequencing in the schema is utility → bill, because the utility question opens the door to 'and what are you paying on average?' Larry answered BGE and the rep moved directly to prior designs. The bill anchor — which the schema calls 'THE anchor for the entire pitch' — was never established. For a garage-only solar install the bill anchor is admittedly non-standard, but the rep didn't even attempt to understand Larry's energy cost motivation.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that a garage-only install doesn't warrant a bill anchor since the garage has no existing meter. Plausible, but the rep never articulated this reasoning and the verify.average_bill item was simply skipped, not consciously bypassed.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to discuss the utility → bill sequencing with the rep. Even on a non-standard install, understanding what Larry is paying (or expects to pay) for the garage's electricity gives the specialist a value anchor. If the rep genuinely believes bill collection is irrelevant for a no-meter garage, that's a product knowledge conversation, not a sequencing fix.

stewart_read: Larry misremembers the company name — he's booking an appointment with a company he can't correctly identify. The rep responded 'Correct.' at 03:09, which ambiguously validates the wrong name rather than gently correcting it. A customer who doesn't know who they're meeting with is a soft no-show risk: when the text arrives from 'ION Solar' it may not match his mental model of the appointment. The schema's intro section emphasizes legitimacy establishment in the first 30 seconds — this moment reveals that legitimacy was not fully anchored.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have heard 'Thomann Solar' as a garbled version of 'Ion Solar' and confirmed to avoid confusion. Even if so, the correct move is a gentle correction, not confirmation of the wrong name.
coaching_implication: Show the rep this exchange. The fix is simple: 'Actually, we're ION Solar — I just want to make sure you've got the right name when we text you.' Thirty seconds of correction here materially reduces no-show risk. Manager should also check whether the intro was clear enough at the top of the call — if Larry never caught the company name, the intro may have been rushed or unclear.

quote: No. I haven't spoke with anyone else yet, but I am gonna call for other references.
stewart_read: Larry signals he's shopping — he hasn't seen other designs yet but intends to. The rep responded 'Okay. Awesome. Yeah. No. Good. Okay. Sweet.' and moved immediately to closing the appointment. Strategic read: the rep correctly prioritized locking the appointment before Larry shops, which is the right instinct — a booked appointment before competitors get in is a real advantage. Reactive read: the rep missed an opportunity to create urgency or differentiation that would make Larry less likely to cancel after talking to competitors. The strategic read is stronger here — booking first is the right move — but the rep left the differentiation moment on the table entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: rep should have used the 'I'm gonna call others' signal to create a brief differentiation hook before booking. Rejected as primary because locking the appointment first is the higher-value move at this stage.
coaching_implication: Affirm the instinct to book before Larry shops. Then ask: what one thing could you have said after booking that would make Larry less likely to cancel after talking to a competitor? The schema's rebuttals.already_saw_design reframe ('it's always best to get a couple looks') could be adapted here as a post-booking inoculation.

quote: Well, yeah, Larry, then we got you all qualified. I have everything I need here on my side.
stewart_read: The rep declares qualification complete after collecting: address (confirmed), utility company (BGE), garage dimensions (26x26), and prior designs (none). Missing: homeownership explicitly confirmed, bill/usage data, credit qualifier, and — most critically — any confirmation that Ion can serve a no-meter detached garage. 'We got you all qualified' is stated as fact when the most important qualification question (product fit for the use case) was never answered.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have had product knowledge that a no-meter garage install is within Ion's capability and considered the qualification complete on that basis. No evidence in the transcript supports this — the rep never stated it.
coaching_implication: This is the call's central coaching moment. Walk the rep through what 'qualified' actually means: homeowner confirmed, credit threshold met, product fit confirmed, bill anchor established. Ask the rep which of those they completed. The goal isn't to shame the rep — it's to build a checklist habit so 'qualified' means something specific, not 'I have enough to book.'

quote: Can you give me a consensus of what it would take to have that minimal amount of solar up there?
stewart_read: Larry is asking a sizing/cost question — this is an enthusiasm signal, not an objection. He's trying to understand if the economics work for his specific use case (minimal solar for a garage with power tools, lights, and outlets). The rep's scope-hold response ('I'm not too sure only because I'm in the scheduling, and design department') is structurally correct per the schema. But the rep missed the enthusiasm embedded in the question: Larry is mentally running the numbers, which means he's already imagining the install. The rep could have acknowledged the curiosity before deflecting.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep handled this correctly by holding scope. The enthusiasm signal read is additive, not contradictory — the rep did the right procedural thing but left the emotional acknowledgment on the table.
coaching_implication: Show the rep that Larry's sizing question is a buying signal. The scope-hold was right. The missed move was a brief acknowledgment before the deflection — something like 'That's exactly the right question, and it's one our specialist can answer precisely for your setup.' The schema's universal escape hatch ('That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') is close to what the rep said but warmer. Manager can use this to reinforce scope discipline while adding the emotional acknowledgment layer.

quote: So if that's a concern of yours, I can go ahead and put it down here in our notes to make sure my specialist goes over it with you.
stewart_read: The rep offers to document Larry's sizing question in the notes — a small but real trust move. It signals to Larry that his question won't be lost in the handoff, and it gives the closer a specific agenda item. This is the rep's best moment in the call: scope held, customer concern acknowledged, handoff continuity created. The execution is slightly passive ('if that's a concern of yours') but the intent is right.
alternative_hypothesis: This could be read as a filler move — the rep saying something to fill the silence after the scope-hold. The note-taking framing makes it more than filler; it's a concrete commitment. Strategic read holds.
coaching_implication: Affirm this move explicitly in the 1-on-1. The rep did something right here that should be reinforced: documenting customer concerns for the specialist is a handoff discipline behavior the schema values. The only refinement is to make it more confident: 'I'm putting that in the notes right now so our specialist comes prepared to answer it specifically for your garage setup.'

quote: We'll shoot you a text a couple of hours prior just to reconfirm that still works and so we're not popping by unannounced.
stewart_read: The rep's button-up is thin: no one-hour block stated, no 'is that fair' handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor (appropriately omitted for an in-person visit). The text-reconfirmation promise is the one structural element that was executed — and it's genuinely valuable given the fragility of this appointment (wrong company name, unresolved product fit, shopping customer). The button-up as a whole is incomplete, but the reconfirmation promise partially compensates by creating a touchpoint before the visit.
alternative_hypothesis: For an in-person visit, some button-up elements (computer, no-driving) are legitimately irrelevant. The missing elements are the time-block anchor and the verbal handshake — those apply regardless of visit format.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the button-up checklist for in-person visits specifically: time block ('I've got you for about an hour'), verbal handshake ('Is that fair?'), and the reconfirmation text. The rep got the text right — build from that. The 'is that fair' handshake is the highest-value missing element given that Larry is a shopping customer who may cancel after talking to competitors.
